title: Different Endings
wikitext:
[]: Different Endings             Quote Post by pwhodges » Wed Oct 09, 2013 7:12 am The original NGE TV series ended with an abrupt scene congratulating Shinji for making his decision. However, people immediately managed to disagree whether that decision was to embrace instrumentality or reject it! So without further ado, there was discussion and argument about which of the two identical endings was the true one... Then there was EoE. A third ending, explicitly different, but for many ambiguous in its intent. Is the ending pointing to an optimistic future, or is it merely representing that Shinji is as screwed up as ever? Is the ending intended to be complementary to the TV series, spelling the same result out differently, or is it a rethinking leading to a new ending? It didn't help that the last words spoken are (a) not what Anno had written, (b) ambiguous in translation, and (c) of unclear import anyway! Skip a decade and more. Now Anno is making a new version of the story; but we have another year or two to wait before we see how he will end it this time. Meanwhile, however, his colleague Sadamoto has at last ended his long-running manga version with a very different type of ending. The interpretations of the series and EoE have been bounced around enough times that no fresh argument remains to be made, though people sometimes like to restate their views. However, the new ending of the manga has been widely, though not universally disliked: Bagheera wrote: Now that's an ending that renders everything that came before meaningless.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 09, 2013 9:03 am Except we see an MPE at the end, which rather soundly shoots the theory in the foot. It's hardtop see how the end could be anything other than a mass mindwipe, and thus not even a proper reset.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Oct 09, 2013 9:06 am As bizarre as EoTV is, it still exists within the context of a reality that includes things like Angels and Evas. So it is fully in continuity with the previous 24 episodes. It does not expose them as a "dream", but simply takes a much different presentational approach, confining it to a psychological realm. There are moments, however, where we get glimpses of the 'real world' (e.g., Misato and Ritsuko's corpses), indicating that something we're not privy to is happening beyond EoTV's confines. Unless handled very carefully, I'm of the opinion that "all just a dream" is one of the ultimate narrative ass-pulls. It is largely "consequence-free" storytelling -- in other words, lazy and dishonest. Not to mention it stretches beyond the edges of credulity the notion of what a dream actually is. These fictional "dreams" will usually not be the surreal, only-makes-sense-to-the-dreamer-at-the-time dreamworlds of real life, but fully realized fictional worlds. Although the dreamer is purportedly the only "real person" present, the other characters will be treated as separate individuals rather than mere extensions of the dreamer, with their own histories, aspirations, and so forth. It makes absolutely no sense for a self-contained dream in which Shinji 'resolves his psychological issues' to be a more-or-less internally consistent science fiction setting with a history that extends to years before his birth. It makes no sense for there to be characters who think and do things independently of him, while having detailed histories and motivations. If this is about Shinji's issues, then why in the world are there sequences about the history of Gehirn? Or ones occurring in the psychological landscapes of Rei and Asuka? Or of Seele (who are more distant from Shinji's world than anyone else in the story) talking amongst themselves? Why are there any scenes where Shinji is absent at all? If Shinji were instead, say, writing a manga for the sake of working out his personal issues, then these details would make sense, but obviously there is no suggestion whatsoever that he is secretly a mangaka burning his feelings onto the page. Additionally, going with the "dream" cop-out, nothing matters in the story beyond Shinji's psychological state at the end. It was all just a means to an end. So, to list a couple of things people like about the manga that are rendered utterly irrelevant: Kaji and his tale don't matter, and everything Rei went through and her relationship with Shinji don't matter. Of course, the same holds true for the ending we did get, wherein the climax leaves the versions of the characters we'd gotten to know back in the wake of the apocalypse, their fates unknown, and rushes ahead to a feel-good "reset"-style ending featuring implied genetically-identical (lol) reincarnations. I wholeheartedly reject the "dream" interpretation, as it's an even worse cop-out than a face value reading. Honestly, Sadamoto's ending seems custom built for those who want to ignore grim realities. It glosses over the aftermath of Third Impact, which Anno had the integrity to shove in our faces. For all we know, the actual Shinji that the story spent all its time with up until then died horribly in a post-apocalyptic wasteland, but Sadamoto doesn't have the nads to address such dire unpleasantries. It seems like he threw in the "reincarnation" mechanic specifically so he could get around it. Clever, no? So, here, everybody, have a bittersweet scene untold years in the future featuring lookalikes of Eva characters! Everybody you actually knew is dead (except Yui and Gendo), but these guys look the same and aren't miserable as far as you know, so don't you just feel great?

[]: Re: Different Endings             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Wed Oct 09, 2013 9:25 am pwhodges wrote: However, in the other interpretation we have a different way to view things. Our protagonist, Shinji, has worked through his psychological issues as described in the story that has played out in his mind. In the end he has resolved these issues and is cured of them, or at least can leave them behind as he moves forward. We see him moving forward in the final stage of the manga, which is in the real world for the first time; the rest of the story is not, except possibly for some of the flashbacks, e.g. to Shinji's earlier schooldays. After the cure, he has no further need of the means, and so he can forget them, as some people forget the details of their childhood, or other experiences, especially if traumatic. Shinji is shown to have residual memories by the sight of the fossilised MP Eva, and his half-recognition of Asuka; though I think those are actually slight, but understandable, weaknesses in how the story is told.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Oct 09, 2013 9:34 am ElMariachi : Where did you get the impression that Second Impact was erased from history? The MP Eva "fossil" in the last chapter indicates that prior events occurred. Yui indicated that Shinji would continue to be reborn, i.e., that reincarnation exists in this universe. Hence, the final chapter exists untold years after the previous one, and features "reincarnations" of the characters who (via some contrived means or another) look exactly the same as they did before.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 09, 2013 10:31 am Yeah, There are things in the manga I prefer but the ending is very much not one of them. It feels like a complete cop-out as Reichu argues, and also in getting there once instrumentality kicks off it seems like Shinji has almost nothing to do with it; Rei just has a monologue and then turns into snow because... reasons? It feels very rushed and not well thought out. EoTV is where it's at. A posthumanist hard takeoff to a shared virtual dreamspace where individuals still exist meaningfully but our cast are no longer emotionally crippled and free of the toils of physical existence. It has to end there because beyond this singularity lays an adventure we simply aren't equipped for.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Oct 09, 2013 10:32 am Reichu wrote: ElMariachi : Where did you get the impression that Second Impact was erased from history? The MP Eva "fossil" in the last chapter indicates that prior events occurred. Yui indicated that Shinji would continue to be reborn, i.e., that reincarnation exists in this universe. Hence, the final chapter exists untold years after the previous one, and features "reincarnations" of the characters who (via some contrived means or another) look exactly the same as they did before.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireball » Wed Oct 09, 2013 10:34 am Manga end is against everything Eva ever stood for. It's pure escapism for the sake of making a happier ending and throwing LAS a bone. Terrible

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Wed Oct 09, 2013 10:44 am Yep it should end with Shinji and Asuka dying in a world usnuitable to live, before anyone (if ever) emerges. That would be much more fitting.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Oct 09, 2013 10:53 am The Manga ending was like an imitation of RahXephon : reset the world, get back to the girl you live, and forget that the others ever existed. Pathetic. Stillborn wrote: Yep it should end with Shinji and Asuka dying in a world usnuitable to live, before anyone (if ever) emerges. That would be much more fitting.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:06 am What downer crap? Fireball complained about escapism. I gave him more realistic alternative. You have two teens in destroyed desolated city. One crippled mentally and emotionally, other physically (and most likely emotionally). They would be very likely awkward towards each other and would have trouble cooperating properly. Asuka would be most likely the driving force, with Shinji stumbling behind like a deadweight, trying to get his mind together. What are the chances of them survivng in this environment, long enough for anyone to emerge in their vicinity, let alone one competent enough to help and assist them to asure mutual survival? The fact that you don't like my opinion doesn't mean it's worthless.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:09 am Stillborn : Sadamoto shouldn't have utilized the EoE framework if he couldn't own up to the consequences of Third Impact. That's all there is to it. Just leaving the original Shinji's fate off the written page and conveniently moving on to some future "reincarnation" is narrative cowardice.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:13 am But Anno did the same, just instead of showing the far future he cut off before thing started to get ugly on the screen.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:19 am Stillborn : All right, you have a point, but the very fact that there's nothing shown after that leaves hope that they can still pick something up out of the mess. Reichu wrote: Just leaving the original Shinji's fate off the written page and conveniently moving on to some future "reincarnation" is narrative cowardice.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:21 am Stillborn wrote: But Anno did the same, just instead of showing the far future he cut off before thing started to get ugly on the screen.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:29 am Sadmoto's ending merely shows that he believes things DO worked out in the future and world rised up from its fall. It shows that humanity did survived and restored itself. What we don't know is the fate of original Shinji and Asuka, and its the same with Anno's ending. Just Anno's ending doesn't left much hope for their survival or for future of human race. I see no reason why Sadamoto's ending should be considered weaker one. Just because it's more happy (at least for mankind in general)?

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:32 am In a sense, YES. It's weak because it's too happy and too simple an ending. The original ending was bleak but ambiguous - you could never know whether or not others would emerge from that ocean and rebuild human civilization. But the Manga ending simply "resets" the world with Shinji having a happy, normal life, which means that everything that Shinji did and suffered for is moot, worthless. Totally against the point of NGE, don't you think?

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:34 am Well, my first thoughts there got pretty comprehensively trampled on ... It may be that Sadamoto's ending works better if you have the Buddhist belief in reincarnation deeply embedded in your world-view (as opposed to merely knowing about it, while is all I, as a westerner, would claim); it might make the transition from his parents' final greetings to the last stage a more obvious and natural progression. And I do wonder what would actually be gained by spelling out the state of the world after 3I - it's already been shown to be in a bad way. Perhaps we should just assume that nothing survives after instrumentality is rejected. Rei rains down on the world to await Shinji's return in the next cycle of existence, which we are then shown. Shinji is shown looking the same not because he would but so that we can recognise him; Asuka the same. I'm not even clear that the "Kensuke" figure is actually meant to be, though, as there's no reason for him to be (unlike Asuka). And Misato's cross at the end is just a blatant "D'aw!" thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:36 am ^^ I don't think. After all Seele is stopped. Their plot is thwarted. Something did progressed for the world. As for Shinji's struggles being meaningless... Isn't that how it usually is with a single human life? It is and then it dies, without any meaning. There, you have your bleakness you missed in Sadamoto's happy ending

[]:             Quote Post by Fireball » Wed Oct 09, 2013 11:37 am Stillborn wrote: What downer crap? Fireball complained about escapism. I gave him more realistic alternative. You have two teens in destroyed desolated city. One crippled mentally and emotionally, other physically (and most likely emotionally). They would be very likely awkward towards each other and would have trouble cooperating properly. Asuka would be most likely the driving force, with Shinji stumbling behind like a deadweight, trying to get his mind together. What are the chances of them survivng in this environment, long enough for anyone to emerge in their vicinity, let alone one competent enough to help and assist them to asure mutual survival? The fact that you don't like my opinion doesn't mean it's worthless.
